title: 23 Tir — St. Solomon & The Assembly
narrative:
narrative": "The twenty-third day of Tir returns to the figure of King Solomon, the son of David, whose wisdom in the Ethiopian tradition is inexhaustible as a source of theological meditation. In the light of Tir, Solomon's prayer at the dedication of the Temple — 'Will God indeed dwell on earth?' — finds its answer not only in the Incarnation of Tahsas but in the Epiphany of Tir: for the God who deigned to dwell in human flesh at Bethlehem has now publicly claimed that flesh as His own dwelling-place at the Jordan, and the Spirit who filled the Temple of Solomon with the divine glory has descended as a dove upon the living Temple of the Lord's body. Solomon's Song of Songs, attributed to him by the tradition, has been read by the fathers as the love poem of the baptized soul and its divine Beloved — and in Tir, the Song is the song of the soul that has heard the Voice from heaven naming it 'beloved' in the person of the Lord and has responded with the full ardor of its longing.

Saint Clement of Ancyra, a bishop-martyr of the early fourth century who endured repeated imprisonments and torments over the course of several decades before his final martyrdom, is commemorated on this day. Clement — whose name means 'merciful' or 'gentle' in the Latin — displayed the gentleness of his name not by avoiding suffering but by bearing it without bitterness or diminishment of charity toward those who inflicted it. The extraordinary duration of his martyrdom — the sources speak of suffering extended across nearly thirty years under multiple emperors — gave it the character of a lifetime ascesis: Clement's martyrdom was itself a form of desert solitude, an enforced withdrawal from the world's comforts into the naked encounter with the God who had promised to be with His witnesses even in the prison and before the tribunal.

Saint Agathangelus, the companion and secretary of Clement of Ancyra who was martyred alongside him, bears a name in the Greek that means 'good angel' or 'one who brings good news' — a name that connects him to the Archangel Gabriel and to the evangelical ministry of proclamation. Agathangelus recorded the acts of Clement's martyrdom and by so doing became himself the bearer of good news: the good news that the suffering of the saints is not meaningless endurance but a participation in the epiphanic act of the Lord at the Jordan, where He too voluntarily submitted to what was beneath His dignity in order to hallow the place of submission for all who would follow Him.

The theological seal of this twenty-third day of Tir is the mystery of the Temple and the living stone. Solomon built a Temple of cedar and gold that could not contain the God it housed; Clement becomes in his martyrdom a living stone in the Temple not made with hands; Agathangelus bears the good news of that living construction to all who will hear it. The Epiphany declares that the God who could not be contained in Solomon's Temple has chosen to dwell in the living temple of human flesh — and the martyrs, who offer that flesh upon the altar of their testimony, are the most literal fulfilment of Solomon's impossible dream: that the Most High should indeed dwell on earth.
